1: Then Moses and the people of Israel sang (az yashir Moshe): Az = then (retrospective poetic formula; the perfect tense embedded in an imperfect form signals the performative, celebratory character of the ode; cf. Numbers 21:17, Joshua 10:12). The Song of the Sea (Shirat HaYam) is among the oldest poetry in the Hebrew Bible, exhibiting archaic grammatical features. For he has triumphed gloriously (ki ga'oh ga'ah): The infinitive absolute + Qal perfect of ga'ah for intensification; ga'ah = to rise up, be exalted, triumph — the verb of majesty and pride used for divine victory. The horse and his rider he has thrown into the sea (ramah): Ramah = to throw/hurl; the conciseness of the image (horse + rider + sea) becomes the song's refrain.
2: YHWH is my strength and my song (ozi ve'zimrat Yah): The first occurrence of Yah — the short form of YHWH — embedded in Hebrew poetry here and in Psalms 118:14 (cited in Isaiah 12:2). Oz = strength/might; zimrat = song/praise, from zamar (to sing/make music). The phrase is not 'he is my strength and I sing about him' but 'he is my strength and my song' — his power is itself what I sing. He has become my salvation (vayyehi-li lishuah): Yeshuah — the noun that names Joshua and Jesus. This is my God (zeh Eli): The demonstrative force of zeh — not an abstract theological proposition but a finger pointed at the one who just split the sea and drowned the army.
3: YHWH is a man of war (ish milchamah): The divine warrior title is explicit and unapologetic; milchamah = war/battle (from lacham = to fight). This is the only place in Scripture where YHWH is directly called a 'man of war'; the image is not domesticated. YHWH is his name (YHWH shemo): The self-identification formula — the victor's name is declared after the victory; the name is proven by the deed.
4: Pharaoh's chariots (markevot Par'oh) and his host (ve'cheilo) he cast (yaroh) into the sea: Yaroh = Qal perfect of yarah (to throw/hurl); the same root as Torah (instruction/direction, from the same throwing/pointing gesture). His chosen officers (mivchar shalishav) were sunk (tuba'u) in Yam Suph: Bacha = to choose/select; shalish = officer of the third rank (possibly chariot commander); taba = to sink/drown.
5: The floods (tehomot) covered them: Tehomot = deep waters, plural of tehom — the primordial deep of Genesis 1:2 (tehom, the abyss over which the Spirit hovered); the waters of chaos/death that YHWH ordered at creation now serve as instruments of judgment on those who opposed the new creation of the Exodus. They went down into the depths (metzulot) like a stone: Metzulot = depths (from tzlul = to sink); the stone image becomes lead in Exodus 15:10.
6: Your right hand (yemincha), O YHWH, glorious in power (ne'dari vakoach): Nedar = to be glorious/distinguished/majestic; the divine right hand (yamin) is the instrument of both creation (Psalms 98:1) and destruction. Your right hand shatters (tir'atz) the enemy: Ratatz = to dash to pieces/shatter (the same verb as Psalms 2:9 — ‘dash them to pieces like a potter’s vessel’).
7: In the greatness of your majesty (berov ga'oncha) you overthrow (teharos) your adversaries: Ga'on = majesty/grandeur, but also pride/arrogance — the word can be used for both divine majesty and human hubris; YHWH's ga'on is what Pharaoh's ga'on was trying to counterfeit. Haras = to pull down, tear down. You send out your fury (charoncha) and it consumes them like stubble (ke'qash): Charon = burning anger (from charah, to burn); the imagery of fire consuming chaff is judgment language used throughout the prophets (Isaiah 5:24; Nahum 1:10).
8: At the blast of your nostrils (beruach appecha): Ruach appecha = the wind/breath of your nostrils — the dual form apayim (nostrils) emphasizes the physical image of YHWH exhaling in anger; the same ruach that moved over the waters in Genesis 1:2. The floods stood upright like a heap (netzvu ke'mo-ned): Natzav = to stand erect/upright (Niphal); ned = a heap or mound of water (also used in Joshua 3:13,16 for the Jordan's walls at the crossing). The deep waters congealed (qafe'u tehomot): Qapha = to congeal/coagulate (of water freezing or thickening) — the tehom of Genesis 1:2, now congealed into solid walls at divine command, anticipates the Jordan crossing and inverts the creation narrative: waters that were separated at creation are re-parted for redemption.
9: The enemy said, 'I will pursue (erdof), I will overtake (asig), I will divide the spoil': The enemies' first-person boast is quoted verbatim — five verbs of self-confident action (pursue, overtake, divide, fill, draw sword, destroy). The boast structure is a rhetorical device in ancient Near Eastern poetry; cf. the Assyrian king's boasts in Isaiah 10:8-14. My desire (nafshi) shall have its fill of them: Nefesh = soul/self/throat/desire; nafshi here = 'my appetite' — the enemy consuming Israel is now consumed by the sea.
10: You blew with your wind (nashafta b'ruacha), the sea covered them: Nashaf = to blow; the divine breath/wind that parted the sea now reverses and closes it. They sank like lead (ka'oferet) in the mighty waters (mayim addirim): Oferet = lead (the heaviest common metal); addirim = mighty/majestic (same root as adon = lord). The progression from stone (Exodus 15:5) to lead (Exodus 15:10) emphasizes the finality of the sinking.
11: Who is like you (mi-kamokha), O YHWH, among the gods (ba'elim)? The incomparability formula — the rhetorical question that functions as the song's theological climax. Ba'elim = among the mighty ones/gods; the song does not deny the existence of other powers but asserts YHWH's incomparable superiority. The name Micah (miykah = who is like YHWH?) derives from this verse. Awesome in glorious deeds (nora tehillot): Nora = awesome/fearsome (Niphal participle of yara = to fear); tehillot = praises/glorious deeds. Doing wonders (oseh-pele'): Pele' = wonder/miracle — the nomen of marvel that defies categorization.
12: You stretched out your right hand; the earth swallowed them (tivelam ha'aretz): Bala' = to swallow/engulf; the earth (aretz) now joins the sea as instrument of judgment — the text moves beyond the historical event to its cosmic significance. The victory over Pharaoh is a defeat of the anti-creation forces.
13: In your covenant love (bechasdecha) you have led (nachita) the people whom you have redeemed (ga'alta): Hesed = covenant faithfulness/steadfast love; nachah = to lead/guide (the shepherd verb); ga'al = to redeem as kinsman-redeemer (the same term as in Exodus 6:6 — YHWH as Israel's go'el). The Exodus is here framed as an act of kinsman-redemption: YHWH acting as the nearest relative who pays the ransom price to free enslaved kin. To your holy abode (neveh qodshecha): Neveh = pasture/abode (the shepherd's dwelling place — the cosmic mountain as YHWH's pasture).
14: The peoples have heard (shamu'u), they tremble (yirgazun): Ragaz = to tremble/shake (with both fear and excitement — the ambiguous trembling of theophany). The ripple effect: Egypt's defeat produces existential terror in all surrounding nations. Anguish has seized the inhabitants of Philistia (chil achaz yoshevei Peleshet): Chil = birth pangs/writhing; achaz = to seize/grip; the nations writhe as if in labor — a frequent prophetic metaphor for divine judgment (Isaiah 13:8; Jeremiah 6:24).
15: The chiefs of Edom (alufei Edom) are dismayed (nibhalu); the leaders of Moab, trembling seizes them (yo'chuzan ra'ad): Aluf = chief/thousand-commander (from aleph = ox = strength); bahel = to be terrified/dismayed; ra'ad = trembling. Edom and Moab are the future enemies Israel will encounter on the way to Canaan — their paralysis is predicted here, to be confirmed in Num 22 (Balak of Moab's terror) and the Edom encounter in Num 20.
16: Terror and dread fall upon them (tippol aleihem emah va'pachad): Emah = terror (from the root of fear); pachad = dread/sudden fear. Because of the greatness of your arm (bigdol zero'echa) they are still as a stone: Zero'a = arm (the arm of YHWH is a major Exodus image alongside the hand/yad); the nations are petrified — literally stone-still. Till the people pass by whom you have purchased (qanita): Qanah = to acquire/create/possess — the creation verb (Genesis 14:19 'Creator/Possessor of heaven and earth' uses qanah). Israel is YHWH's possession in the sense of being his created handiwork.
17: You will bring them in and plant them (tebitemo v'tita'emo) on your own mountain (behar nachalotecha): Neta' = to plant; nahalah = inheritance/allotment (the land-distribution term that will structure Joshua). The planting of Israel on the mountain is the agricultural image of settlement. The sanctuary (miqdash) which your hands have established (konenut yadecha): Miqdash = holy place; kun = to establish/prepare. The Song of the Sea anticipates both Sinai (the immediate destination) and Zion (the ultimate destination), with the tabernacle as the mobile version and the Temple as the fixed form.
18: YHWH will reign forever and ever (YHWH yimloch le'olam va'ed): Malak = to reign/be king (Qal imperfect — ongoing, permanent reign); olam = age/eternity; va'ed = and beyond/and further. The concluding declaration of the Song: the Exodus has demonstrated YHWH's eternal kingship. This verse is embedded in Jewish liturgy (Aleinu prayer) as the eschatological hope; Revelation's heavenly hymns draw on this declaration (Revelation 11:15; Exodus 19:6).
19: For when the horses of Pharaoh came with his chariots and horsemen into the sea, YHWH brought back the waters of the sea over them: The prose summary (Exodus 15:19-21) provides the historical referent for the poetry of Exodus 15:1-18, framing the Song as the inspired response to a specific historical event. The prose restates the key facts without embellishment, grounding the poem in history.
20: Miriam the prophetess (Miriam hanevi'ah), the sister of Aaron: Miriam is identified as a prophetess (nevi'ah) — the feminine form of navi, one who speaks for God. She is named sister of Aaron (not of Moses), possibly reflecting a tradition that distinguishes her primary association. Took a tambourine (tof) in her hand: Tof = hand-drum/tambourine (the standard instrument of female celebration in ancient Israel — cf. Judges 11:34; 1 Samuel 18:6); with dancing (b'machol): machol = round dance (from chul = to whirl/dance). The women's antiphonal response was a recognized form of celebration after military victory.
21: Miriam sang to them: 'Sing to YHWH, for he has triumphed gloriously': The Miriam Song (Exodus 15:21) is possibly the oldest literary layer — a two-line refrain that Moses's longer song (Exodus 15:1-18) elaborates. The relationship between the two songs (which is earlier?) is debated; the antiphonal structure (Moses's song → Miriam's response) may reflect the liturgical performance tradition of Israel's victory celebrations. Both use the same opening (ki ga'oh ga'ah), suggesting either direct quotation or a shared liturgical formula.
22: Moses led Israel onward from Yam Suph (vayyasa' Moshe et-Yisra'el): The Hiphil of nasa' (to cause to journey) — Moses as the human shepherd/leader. Wilderness of Shur (midbar Shur): Shur = wall (possibly the Egyptian border wall, known archaeologically as the 'Wall of the Ruler' or 'Walls of Horus' — the defensive frontier). The wilderness of Shur is the Sinai Peninsula's western desert. Three days without finding water: The three-day journey that tests the new nation immediately after the Song — from triumph to thirst in three days.
23: Marah — they could not drink (lo yaklu lishhot) the water because it was bitter (ki marim hem): Marah means bitter; the place is named from the water's quality (aetiological etymology). Mar = bitter; the narrative names the place by the crisis it creates. The bitter-waters test follows immediately upon the crossing, establishing the wilderness complaint pattern: abundance and crisis alternate to develop trust.
24: The people grumbled (vayillonu) against Moses: 'What shall we drink?' Lun = to murmur/lodge/complain (the technical wilderness-complaint verb; also used in Exodus 16:2, Exodus 17:3; Numbers 14:2; etc.). The complaint is against Moses but addressed to YHWH — the people's anger at the human leader is always implicitly against the divine one who sent him. The question mah nishteh (what shall we drink?) is the first of the wilderness water-complaints.
25: He cried to YHWH (vayyitz'aq el-YHWH), and YHWH showed him a log (etz): Za'aq = to cry out (Moses uses the same verb the Israelites used in Exodus 2:23 — the leader intercedes using the vocabulary of oppression). Etz = tree/wood/timber — the material is deliberately unspecified; the tradition does not explain what species of tree or how the chemistry works, preserving the miraculous character of the sweetening. The etz thrown into bitter water to make it sweet is a type-pattern rich in later typology: the cross (the etz of crucifixion) makes the bitter waters of existence sweet. There he made for them a statute and a rule (choq umishpat) and there he tested them (nissahu): First occurrence of choq (statute/ordinance) and mishpat (judgment/rule) together — the pair that will structure Sinai legislation. Nissah = to test (the verb of Genesis 22:1 — the Aqedah); the wilderness is the testing ground where faith formed at the sea is refined.
26: If you will diligently listen (shamo'a tishma') to the voice of YHWH: The doubled infinitive absolute + imperfect = the most emphatic form of the conditional — 'if you will truly/completely listen.' And do what is right in his eyes (hay'yashar b'einav): Yashar = upright/straight/right; the ethical standard is YHWH's sight, not human consensus. I will put none of the diseases (machalah) of Egypt upon you: The diseases of Egypt = the plagues (the ten plagues are now a threat to Israel if they disobey — the covenant has two sides). For I am YHWH your healer (YHWH rofecha): Rapha = to heal; YHWH-Rophe' = YHWH the Healer — the first compound divine name in the post-Exodus narrative. The name reveals YHWH's character through the act: the God who made bitter water sweet is also the God who heals the diseases of Egypt. Healing and obedience are structurally linked.
27: Twelve springs of water (shtem esreh einot mayim) and seventy palm trees (shiv'im temarim): Twelve springs (ayin = spring/eye) correspond to the twelve tribes — the abundant provision matches the structure of the community. Seventy palms (temarim = date palms, from tamar = upright/palm) correspond to the seventy souls who went down to Egypt (Genesis 46:27) and the seventy elders of Israel. The numbers are too precise to be accidental; Elim is a type of the eschatological rest — twelve wells and seventy trees, enough for each tribe and each elder. They encamped there by the water (al-hamayim): After bitter Marah, abundant Elim — the rhythm of trial and provision that will structure the entire wilderness narrative.
